original_prompt: Am I free for lunch at 12:00 on 2024-05-19? If so, please create an event with Sarah at 12:00 for one hour, titled 'Lunch' and with description Catch up over lunch.. Her email address is 'sarah.connor@gmail.com'.
assessment_reasoning: The prompt clearly specifies the day and time to check (2024-05-19 at 12:00), the conditional action (“If so”), and all event details needed to create the event (title, description, start time, one-hour duration/end time derivable, and participant email). The correct first tool call (retrieve that day’s calendar events) is unambiguous and the event creation parameters are present; only minor runtime details (e.g., timezone or invite/accept behavior) are unspecified but do not change the first tool path or required data.

exposure_request: Am I free for lunch at 12:00 on 2024-05-19?
semantic_cues: Please give me their email addresses
goal_cues: Please give me their email addresses

natural_level_plan.rationale: The task naturally supports a small progression of ambiguity along the axes of (1) whether the user asks only to check availability vs. also to create an event, (2) presence/absence of explicit event details (duration, title) and (3) presence/absence of the event description. All levels keep the temporal cue that triggers a day-level calendar lookup and the core participant/title/email references so the first tool call remains get_day_calendar_events(day='2024-05-19').

levels.description: Minimal availability check. Contains the date/time, the 'Lunch' cue, and Sarah's email, but does not request creating an event.
must_include: Ask if I'm free for lunch at 12:00 on 2024-05-19

levels.description: Availability check plus a conditional create request. Specifies creating an event with Sarah at 12:00 for one hour, titled 'Lunch', but omits the event description.
must_include: Ask if I'm free for lunch at 12:00 on 2024-05-19
should_omit: Event description text (Catch up over lunch.)

natural_levels.N1: Am I free for lunch at 12:00? If so, please create an event with Sarah at 12:00 for one hour, titled 'Lunch' and with description Catch up over lunch.. Her email address is 'sarah.connor@gmail.com'.
natural_levels.N2: Am I free for lunch at 12:00 on 2024-05-19? If so, please create a one-hour event at 12:00 with Sarah (sarah.connor@gmail.com) titled 'Lunch'.

N1.reasoning: The prompt clearly implies the correct first tool: check the calendar for a free slot at 12:00 (get_day_calendar_events) and, if free, create an event with the given title, description, duration, and participant email (create_calendar_event). It preserves the right object/source (the user's calendar) and almost all event details (time, duration, title, description, participant). Major missing operational detail: which day/date (and implicitly timezone) to check/create the event for. That omission leaves non-trivial uncertainty about the first tool call's day argument, so the prompt is very ambiguous but still workable.

N2.reasoning: The prompt clearly preserves the correct first tool path: check the calendar for events on 2024-05-19 (get_day_calendar_events) to verify availability at 12:00, then create a one-hour event if free. It supplies the date, start time, duration, title, and participant email, so the primary object/source of truth (the user's calendar for that day) is unambiguous. Minor operational uncertainties remain (time zone assumptions, which calendar/account to use if multiple, and unspecified event description), but none change the first-tool choice or block execution, so the instruction is actionable with only small implementation details left. Calibration adjusted the score from 4 to 5 based on preserved specificity cues.
